call_meta.crm: HubSpot (migrated from Pipedrive within last year)
next_steps: Schedule POC success criteria call for April 29
Set up trial environment for hands-on evaluation
Prepare custom video demos for specific use cases if requested

jtbd.job: Bring quoting into HubSpot so pricing data flows back to the CRM without manual re-entry
jtbd.context: Sales reps currently build quotes in DocuSign, which is completely disconnected from HubSpot. Nothing written in DocuSign (line items, pricing, totals) flows back into the CRM. Reps must manually enter deal amounts a second time.
jtbd.desired_outcome: Quotes created inside HubSpot with line items, deal amounts, and product selections automatically synced to the deal record

jtbd.job: Enforce pricing policies and guardrails across 200 sales reps in 25 countries without manual oversight
jtbd.context: No approval workflows exist today. Reps discount on the fly and CC managers informally. RevOps has zero visibility into what pricing is actually being offered. Multiple currencies (EUR, DKK) with different price books per country.
jtbd.desired_outcome: Automated price book assignment by country/currency, discount guardrails that trigger approvals only when thresholds are exceeded, and full visibility into what is being quoted

jtbd.job: Standardize and automate quote template distribution across 25 country offices
jtbd.context: 80 DocuSign templates managed manually. Reps request templates by country, and the RevOps specialist shares them individually. Semi-automated but still requires manual intervention for every new rep or country change.
jtbd.desired_outcome: Rules-based template assignment where the correct template is automatically selected based on customer country, deal type, or other HubSpot properties

pains.pain: Quoting process completely disconnected from CRM — nothing flows back to HubSpot
pains.trigger: Every quote created in DocuSign. Reps enter pricing, products, and totals in DocuSign, then must manually re-enter the deal amount in HubSpot. Line items and product details do not sync.
pains.cost: Double data entry on every deal. Inaccurate deal amounts due to manual errors. RevOps cannot report on what is actually being quoted. No product-level revenue visibility.
pains.urgency_reason: This is the primary driver for evaluation. Affects every deal across 200 quoters.

pains.pain: No pricing controls or discount governance — reps set prices manually with no guardrails
pains.trigger: Every quote. Reps manually enter pricing from a price book spreadsheet and can set any price they want. Discounts happen informally with a CC to a sales manager. No systematic approval workflow.
pains.cost: Unknown margin erosion from uncontrolled discounting. No visibility into discount patterns. Management cannot identify problematic pricing until after deals close.
pains.urgency_reason: Executive leadership has no access to pricing and discounting data. Revenue leakage is unmeasured.

pains.pain: Manual price book lookups across multiple currencies create calculation errors and inconsistencies
pains.trigger: Every quote involving tiered pricing. Reps must look up the correct tier in a spreadsheet based on construction cost or annual turnover, apply the right currency (EUR vs. DKK), and manually calculate the price. Different reps enter values differently (e.g., '80 mil' vs. '80,000,000').
pains.cost: Pricing discrepancies from manual lookups. Inconsistent data entry formats. Time wasted on manual calculations that could be automated.
pains.urgency_reason: Causes ongoing friction and errors but not a dealbreaker on its own. Compounds with the lack of guardrails.

pains.pain: 80 DocuSign templates managed manually, distributed by hand across 25 country offices
pains.trigger: Every new rep onboarding or country expansion. The RevOps specialist must manually share the correct templates with each rep based on their country. Template requests come through a semi-automated process but still require manual fulfillment.
pains.cost: RevOps time spent on template administration instead of building scalable processes. Risk of reps using wrong templates for their country.
pains.urgency_reason: Operational burden but has a semi-automated workaround in place.

pains.pain: No reporting or visibility into quoting activity, deal composition, or pricing patterns
pains.trigger: Any time leadership asks about pipeline composition, discount trends, or product mix. The data simply does not exist in the CRM because quoting happens outside of it.
pains.cost: Strategic decisions made without data. Cannot identify which products sell together, which reps discount most, or which regions have margin issues.
pains.urgency_reason: Executive leadership explicitly identified lack of visibility as a critical gap.

pains.pain: Product selection on deals is unreliable — reps select products manually and make mistakes
pains.trigger: When reps choose products on the HubSpot deal record via a selection field, separate from the quoting process. The product selection is disconnected from the actual quote, so mismatches are common.
pains.cost: Inaccurate product data in CRM. Cannot reliably report on product attach rates or product mix across deals.
pains.urgency_reason: Acknowledged pain but lower priority than pricing and visibility gaps.

workflow_reality.current_process: Reps work in HubSpot for deal management but switch to DocuSign for quoting. From a deal record, they click the DocuSign tab, select a country-specific template (from 80 options shared manually by RevOps), fill in company information and pricing manually, look up prices in a spreadsheet price book, calculate totals by hand, and send the quote for signature. After the quote is sent, they return to HubSpot and manually enter the total MRR as the deal amount. No line items, products, or pricing details sync back.
tools_in_use: HubSpot (CRM — migrated from Pipedrive within last year)
Spreadsheet price books (tiered pricing lookup by currency and project cost)
Manual deal fields in HubSpot (product selection, deal amount)

pricing_models.name: Project License
pricing_models.description: Price based on total construction cost tier. Different tiers for EUR and DKK. Five products with tiered pricing tables.
pricing_models.complexity: Table lookup by construction cost range, per currency

pricing_models.name: Company Agreement
pricing_models.description: Price based on annual company turnover. Turnover divided by 12, then multiplied by a per-mil calculation factor (e.g., 3 per mil or 5 per mil depending on product).

brittleness_indicators: 200 reps entering pricing manually with no validation
Reps enter construction costs in inconsistent formats ('80 mil' vs. '80,000,000')
Product selection on deals is unreliable and disconnected from actual quotes
Template distribution is a manual, one-by-one process
No way to audit what pricing was actually quoted vs. what is in the CRM

evaluated.name: Other CPQ tools (unnamed)
evaluated.why_they_passed: Tools were doing quoting outside of HubSpot, requiring integration back to the CRM. Prospect explicitly wants a HubSpot-native solution to avoid maintaining a separate system.
evaluated.key_quote: I think you'd mentioned you were kind of looking at some other tools, but they're still doing a lot of the things outside of your HubSpot, so you're still having to integrate back into the CRM, which you don't want.

evaluated.name: DocuSign
evaluated.what_they_liked: Signing order control (customer signs first, then manager, or vice versa). Customer-editable fields on quotes. Template-based document creation. Familiar to the team.
evaluated.why_they_passed: Completely disconnected from CRM. No pricing logic or calculations. No data flows back to HubSpot. Manual everything.
evaluated.key_quote: None of this gets written back into HubSpot, so I only see once they send this.

evaluated.name: HubSpot native quoting
evaluated.why_they_passed: Could not support their pricing logic (tiered by construction cost, formula-based by turnover, multiple currencies with different price books).
evaluated.key_quote: The reason why you did it in DocuSign is because HubSpot couldn't support your pricing logic.

current_workaround.description: DocuSign for quoting with 80 manually-managed templates, spreadsheet price books for tier lookups, manual deal amount entry in HubSpot
current_workaround.adequacy: Functional but produces no usable data. RevOps and leadership are flying blind on pricing, discounting, and product mix.

quotes.quote: Everything here is manually being put in by the salespeople.
quotes.usage_notes: Clean summary of the core problem. Useful for content about manual quoting pain in mid-market companies.

quotes.quote: Right now you don't really have visibility on your side on really any type of pricing controls and really what's being sold.
quotes.usage_notes: AE restatement of prospect pain. Useful as a setup for the problem statement but not a direct prospect quote.

quotes.quote: None of this gets written back into HubSpot, so I only see once they send this.
quotes.usage_notes: Captures the CRM disconnect pain precisely. Strong for content about data integrity and RevOps frustration with disconnected tools.

quotes.quote: I have to share those manually with people depending on what country they are in. So that's one of my pain points.
quotes.usage_notes: Self-identified pain point around template management at scale. Good for content targeting RevOps personas managing multi-country operations.

quotes.quote: They put the product name, so stadium, and then the fee should be based in our price book. So let me pull that up.
quotes.usage_notes: Shows the manual price book lookup workflow. Useful for illustrating the gap between spreadsheet pricing and automated price books.

quotes.quote: That's already where the discrepancies are coming in.
quotes.usage_notes: Prospect acknowledges that inconsistent manual entry creates pricing discrepancies. Good proof point for data integrity content.

quotes.quote: Of course, that can also be misleading, sometimes not accurate, I would say. Because sometimes people make mistakes.
quotes.usage_notes: Acknowledges that manual product selection is unreliable. Supports content about the cost of human error in quoting.

quotes.quote: I can feel it in your voice, like you can just sense the disconnect between your CRM and your actual quoting process.
quotes.usage_notes: SE observation, not a prospect quote. Captures emotional tone but limited content utility.

quotes.quote: You're basically trusting your sales reps 100% to make sure that they don't make mistakes or that they put the right deal amount in. There's just no integration there at all.
quotes.usage_notes: SE restatement of the guardrails gap. Useful as a problem framing device in content.

quotes.quote: If they are updating their CRM, they have to enter that data twice, which is probably annoying. — Very much so.
quotes.usage_notes: Prospect confirms double data entry pain with emphatic agreement. Strong for content about CRM data integrity and rep productivity.

quotes.quote: So far pretty straightforward, just what I was hoping for with the price books and pricing on this. It looks like with pricing methods and all of this, you would be able to accommodate exactly what we need.
quotes.usage_notes: Strong positive validation of Quotivity price book capabilities. Useful for case study or testimonial content around complex pricing support.

quotes.quote: We need to get our hands on it, right? That's when you find all the niches, little things.
quotes.usage_notes: Indicates evaluation style: hands-on trial is critical to buying decision. Useful for understanding RevOps buyer behavior.

quotes.quote: It's gonna create different problems, but those are the good problems.
quotes.usage_notes: Prospect is mentally past 'if' and into 'when.' Strong positive buying signal. Could be used in content about the mindset shift from manual to automated quoting.

deviant_terms.meaning: A per-mil multiplier applied to annual turnover to determine product pricing in their company agreement model
deviant_terms.frequency: used once but central to their pricing methodology

deviant_terms.meaning: Refers to inconsistent manual data entry formats (e.g., '80 mil' vs. '80,000,000') that cause pricing errors
deviant_terms.frequency: used once, reveals a known systemic problem

deviant_terms.meaning: Problems that come from adopting a better system versus the current problems from having no system. Indicates positive buying intent.

keyword_candidates.source: Core need — prospect evaluated and rejected HubSpot native quoting because it cannot handle tiered or formula-based pricing across multiple currencies
keyword_candidates.content_angle: Why HubSpot's built-in quoting breaks when you have tiered pricing, multi-currency price books, and formula-based calculations

keyword_candidates.source: Direct requirement — prospect needs EUR and DKK price books with different pricing tiers per currency, assigned automatically by customer country
keyword_candidates.content_angle: How to manage multi-currency price books in HubSpot for international sales teams

keyword_candidates.source: Stated pain — no discount controls exist today, reps discount freely, leadership wants automated approvals that only fire when thresholds are exceeded
keyword_candidates.content_angle: How to set up tiered discount approvals in HubSpot that protect margins without slowing down reps

keyword_candidates.source: Direct competitive replacement — prospect is actively moving away from DocuSign for quoting because it is disconnected from their CRM
keyword_candidates.content_angle: Moving from DocuSign to HubSpot-native quoting: what you gain when your quotes live in your CRM

keyword_candidates.source: Industry-specific — prospect is a construction tech SaaS company with project-based pricing tied to total construction cost
keyword_candidates.content_angle: How construction and AEC companies configure project-based pricing in HubSpot

keyword_candidates.keyword: CPQ quote template management at scale
keyword_candidates.source: Operational pain — 80 templates managed manually, distributed by hand to reps across 25 countries
keyword_candidates.content_angle: How RevOps teams manage dozens of quote templates across multiple countries without losing their minds

keyword_candidates.keyword: HubSpot quoting line items not syncing
keyword_candidates.source: Specific technical pain — quotes created in DocuSign do not write line items back to HubSpot deals, forcing manual re-entry
keyword_candidates.content_angle: Why your HubSpot deal data is wrong (and how to fix the quoting-to-CRM gap)

keyword_candidates.source: Executive pain — leadership has no visibility into what is being quoted, discounted, or sold until deals close
keyword_candidates.content_angle: Why your sales leaders can't see what's being quoted (and what that blindness is costing you)
